critic_raw:
This experiment tests a hybrid CNN-RNN model against logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction. Several structural problems undermine the interpretability of any result.

The CNN-RNN architecture processes 'temporal sequences of treatments (procedures, medications)' through a CNN before an LSTM. But the CNN is applying 1D convolutions over a treatment sequence — it acts as a local pattern extractor over adjacent treatments in time. For clinical treatment sequences, local temporal adjacency in the ordered list of procedures is not the same as temporal adjacency in calendar time: two procedures may be listed sequentially because they are co-administered, not because one predicts the other. This is a fundamental mismatch between the architecture's inductive bias (local temporal patterns matter) and the clinical data generating process.

The logistic regression baseline explicitly excludes 'raw sequences, which logistic regression cannot process natively.' This means the comparison is not between architectures on identical inputs — it is between a model with sequence information and a model without. The design states this makes 'any performance gap attributable to the hybrid model's architecture,' but this is precisely wrong: any gap is attributable to the combination of architecture and information advantage. The design would need to include a sequence-unrolled feature set for logistic regression (e.g., time-aggregated statistics of the sequence) to isolate architectural contribution.

The dataset spans 5 calendar years (2019–2023). The test set covers September 2022–December 2023. This 16-month test window spans periods of substantial healthcare disruption (post-COVID policy normalization, staffing patterns). These are not random noise around a stable process — they are systematic shifts that affect both the distribution of treatments and the readmission outcome. The temporal split is appropriate in form, but the claim that performance on this specific test window generalizes to the deployment scenario is questionable without a sensitivity analysis showing performance stability across sub-periods of the test window.

The department-level readmission baseline feature is computed from training data and included in both models. This is sound in principle, but for small departments (n < 500, flagged as a limitation), this estimate will be noisy and may introduce instability in the logistic regression baseline in particular — which relies on this feature as a linear predictor — while the CNN-RNN may be more robust to the noise via its nonlinear processing. This asymmetry is not addressed.

The primary metric is AUPRC, not AUROC. AUPRC is sensitive to the positive class prevalence used during evaluation. If the test set readmission rate drifts from the training rate (a real possibility given the temporal split and healthcare disruptions in the test period), AUPRC point estimates are not directly comparable across time periods, because the denominator of average precision shifts. This is not acknowledged.
defender_raw:
The CNN-RNN hybrid design for readmission prediction is well-constructed. The temporal split by calendar date (Jan 2019–Dec 2021 training, Jan–Aug 2022 validation, Sep 2022–Dec 2023 test) reflects deployment reality and prevents label leakage from future admission records into training. Within-window stratification by hospital department is a notable methodological choice that goes beyond simple temporal splitting: department-level variability is a known confound in readmission prediction (emergency versus elective patients have systematically different risk profiles), and balancing department representation across splits prevents one split from being dominated by low-complexity departments.

The feature engineering is domain-appropriate. The inclusion of department-level readmission baseline rates (computed from training data only) as a feature is particularly sound: it allows both the CNN-RNN and the logistic regression baseline to learn department-specific risk adjustments from a single scalar rather than requiring the model to infer this from patient-level ICD codes alone. This is a fair feature available to both models and directly encodes a known structural confound.

The CNN-RNN architecture is appropriately specified for sequential treatment data: CNN blocks extract local temporal patterns from procedure/medication sequences, and the LSTM captures longer-range dependencies. The explicit architectural description (2 CNN blocks, 1 LSTM layer, 2 dense layers with specified units and dropout) allows reproducibility and critique.

The baseline receives genuine equivalence: the same engineered features (excluding raw sequences, which logistic regression cannot process), grid search over regularization strength with the same objective, and identical preprocessing. The stated rationale — "any performance gap attributable to the hybrid model's architecture rather than tuning disparity" — is the correct framing.

AUPRC as the primary metric is defensible for this imbalanced setting (15–20% positive rate). AUPRC focuses on the positive class and directly reflects the clinical tradeoff between precision (false alarm cost) and recall (missed high-risk patient cost), which aligns with hospital administrators' resource-constrained intervention programs. The secondary SHAP/permutation importance analysis addresses interpretability requirements.

The sensitivity analysis at the department level on the test set is a good control: it checks whether the hybrid model improves performance uniformly or improves some departments while degrading others, which would indicate spurious generalization.
all_issues_raised: The CNN component applies local convolutions over ordered treatment sequences, but clinical treatment ordering reflects co-administration and documentation order rather than causal temporal adjacency — the architecture's inductive bias does not match the data generating process.

The logistic regression baseline excludes raw sequence data, meaning the comparison conflates architectural advantage with information advantage; without a sequence-feature-aggregated logistic regression, the performance gap cannot be attributed to the CNN-RNN architecture.

The test period (September 2022–December 2023) overlaps with systematic post-COVID healthcare shifts, meaning the test distribution is not a stable sample from the deployment scenario; no sub-period stability analysis is described.

The department-level readmission rate feature is noisy for small departments (n < 500), which differentially benefits nonlinear models over logistic regression — this asymmetric noise sensitivity is uncontrolled.

AUPRC is used as the primary metric but is sensitive to positive class prevalence; if readmission rates drift across the temporal split (a likely consequence of the test period's healthcare context), AUPRC point estimates are not directly comparable between the two models.
all_issues_adjudicated: The CNN component applies local convolutions over ordered treatment sequences, but clinical treatment ordering reflects co-administration and documentation order rather than causal temporal adjacency — the architecture's inductive bias does not match the data generating process.

The test period (September 2022–December 2023) overlaps with systematic post-COVID healthcare shifts, meaning the test distribution is not a stable sample from the deployment scenario; no sub-period stability analysis is described.

The department-level readmission rate feature is noisy for small departments (n < 500), which differentially benefits nonlinear models over logistic regression — this asymmetric noise sensitivity is uncontrolled.

AUPRC is used as the primary metric but is sensitive to positive class prevalence; if readmission rates drift across the temporal split (a likely consequence of the test period's healthcare context), AUPRC point estimates are not directly comparable between the two models.
